title: [v6x followup] ChapterSummaryPopover swallows Venice errors
description:
The 'Generate summary' / 'Regenerate' buttons in ChapterSummaryPopover call useSummariseChapterMutation but the popover only renders mutation.isPending. When the mutation rejects (Venice 4xx/5xx, rate-limited, key invalid, balance, malformed response, capability gate, empty chapter, etc.), summariseMutation.isError + summariseMutation.error are ignored, so the popover snaps back from 'Generating…' to the previous state with no UI feedback. User sees nothing.

Fix: render <VeniceErrorBanner> on isError (same pattern as ContinueWriting.tsx:182-197 — code/message/retryAfterSeconds/veniceMessage all available on the ApiError body), with onRetry re-firing summariseMutation.mutate(modelId) and reset() clearing on close/successful re-fire. Storybook story + a test for the banner branch.
notes:
plan: trivial — wire VeniceErrorBanner onto isError branch in frontend/src/components/ChapterSummaryPopover.tsx, mirror ContinueWriting.tsx error mapping (code/message/retryAfterSeconds/veniceMessage from ApiError.body), onRetry re-fires mutation, onDismiss calls mutation.reset(). Add a vitest covering the banner branch (mock summarise POST → 429 with retryAfterSeconds). No backend changes. PUT /summary path (sheet) already surfaces errors via its existing formError text — no change there.

verify: npm --prefix frontend run typecheck && npm --prefix frontend test -- --run tests/components/ChapterSummaryPopover.test.tsx && npx --prefix /home/asg/projects/story-editor biome check frontend/src/components/ChapterSummaryPopover.tsx frontend/tests/components/ChapterSummaryPopover.test.tsx

title: Seed-script subprocess can't resolve story-editor-shared after at5 (PR #106 CI [E12] failure)
description:
## Why

story-editor-at5 made the backend dev server + both vitest suites resolve story-editor-shared from shared/src (exports `source` condition + vitest resolve.alias), and removed every shared/dist build from dev/test/CI (Makefile watcher, dev Dockerfile stage, CI `Build shared` step). But the [E12] encryption-leak test spawns prisma/seed.ts as a subprocess via `npx ts-node` (backend/tests/security/encryption-leak.test.ts). That child process gets neither the vitest alias nor the backend `dev` script's --conditions=source, so it resolves story-editor-shared via the package's `default` export -> shared/dist/index.js — which CI no longer builds. PR #106's CI `lint · typecheck · test` job fails with `Cannot find module '.../story-editor-shared/dist/index.js'`. Same break is latent in `make seed` (spawns the seed inside the dev container, which at5's Task 4 stopped building shared/dist in).

at5's Task 5 premise ('nothing in CI consumes shared/dist') missed this subprocess. Got past spec + code-quality + diff review.

## What

Give the seed-script subprocess the --conditions=source opt-in, the same mechanism the backend `dev` script already uses:
- backend/tests/security/encryption-leak.test.ts: add NODE_OPTIONS=--conditions=source to the env the test passes to the spawned seed (preserving any existing NODE_OPTIONS).
- Makefile `seed` target: pass -e NODE_OPTIONS=--conditions=source to the `docker compose exec` that runs the seed.

Keeps at5's 'no shared/dist in dev/test/CI' property intact. Superseded by story-editor-8i9 (Option D) when shared is bundled into the prod artifact.
notes:
plan: trivial — 2-change CI-regression fix scoped by the user (least-changes Option B); skips /bd-execute per the CLAUDE.md one-off-fix path, straight to /bd-close-reviewed.
verify: rm -rf shared/dist && make stop && make dev && timeout 180 bash -c 'until docker compose exec -T backend wget -qO- http://localhost:4000/api/health 2>/dev/null | grep -q status; do sleep 3; done' && npm -w story-editor-backend run test -- tests/security/encryption-leak.test.ts && npm -w story-editor-shared run build && make stop
close_reason: Seed-script subprocess now gets NODE_OPTIONS=--conditions=source at both spawn sites (the [E12] encryption-leak test and the make seed docker exec), so it resolves story-editor-shared from shared/src instead of the unbuilt shared/dist. Verify ran the [E12] suite with shared/dist deleted — both tests pass. Fixes the PR #106 CI regression introduced by story-editor-at5.

title: [Auth] Cross-account data leak on session switch — TanStack cache + Zustand stores not reset on login/logout
description:
The TanStack Query cache and several Zustand stores retain the previous user's data after a session switch. Until the browser is manually refreshed, user B sees user A's stories, chapters, chats, and (briefly) any plaintext narrative content that was held in client state.

## Reproduction (reported by user)

1. Log in as `demo` user; navigate to a story; let the editor populate (stories list, chapter list, chats).
2. Click "Sign out" or otherwise return to /login.
3. Log in as a different user.
4. **Bug:** the new user sees the demo user's stories / chapters / chats until manual browser refresh.

## Root cause

`frontend/src/hooks/useAuth.ts:95-105` (`login`) and `frontend/src/hooks/useAuth.ts:121-129` (`logout`) do **not** clear the TanStack Query cache. `useSessionStore.clearSession()` (`frontend/src/store/session.ts:44-47`) only clears the access token + user record + status — it has no awareness of the QueryClient. So after logout/login, cached entries `[stories, list]`, `[story, id]`, `[chapter, id]`, `[chats, chapterId]`, `[messages, chatId]`, etc. survive into the next session.

The asymmetry is visible in the codebase: `useDeleteAccountMutation.onSuccess` at `frontend/src/hooks/useAccount.ts:104-108` does the right thing (`queryClient.clear(); clearSession(); navigate(...)`); the other three auth-transition sites (login, logout, signOutEverywhere) skip the cache clear.

## Wider scope — Zustand stores hold plaintext narrative content

Several stores retain per-user plaintext that survives the session swap. Same shape of leak as the cache, but each one is plaintext narrative content from user A:

- `attachedSelection.text` — chapter selection text user A attached to a chat.
- `inlineAIResult.text` / `.output` — selected text + AI completion output.
- `composerDraft.draft` — chat composer draft.
- `chatDraft.drafts[*]` — in-flight chat turns including attachment.selectionText + accumulating assistantText.
- `selection.text` — current TipTap selection.

Identifier-only stores (no plaintext, but reference user A's rows; UX glitch / 404s):

- `activeChapter.activeChapterId`
- `selectedCharacter.selectedCharacterId`
- `charRefSuggestion`
- `errors` (error queue may contain plaintext from user A's error messages)

UI-only stores that are fine to keep:
- `ui.layout`
- `sidebarTab`
- `session` (the whole point — replaced by the new login)

## Proposed fix

Centralized `resetClientState(queryClient: QueryClient)` helper that:
1. Calls `queryClient.clear()`.
2. Resets every per-user Zustand store to its initial state (the stores already expose `clear()` or equivalent setters; otherwise add a setState reset).
3. Is invoked from every auth-transition site: `useAuth.login()` (before `setSession`), `useAuth.logout()` (alongside `clearSession`), `useSignOutEverywhereMutation.onSuccess`, and `useDeleteAccountMutation.onSuccess` (already clears cache; switch to the helper for symmetry).

Optional: also call from `handleUnauthorizedAccess` (`store/session.ts:68-75`) to harden the terminal-401 path. Currently any data refetch from there would refetch over A's stale cache; cache hits would not. Lower-priority but cheap.

## Acceptance

- After login → log out → login as different user, the new user sees only their data (no stale stories / chapters / chats).
- Plaintext narrative content from user A is not retained in any client store after auth transition.
- No browser refresh needed.
- Integration test: TanStack Query cache is empty after each of the four auth-transition paths (login, logout, signOutEverywhere, deleteAccount).
- Unit tests on the helper assert each Zustand store transitions to its initial-state value.

## Related

- story-editor-3y0 (open PR #93) — different surface, also a frontend privacy/UX bug. Stack on top of `convergence` (after PR #93 + PR #92 merge).
- CLAUDE.md "Security Review" — auth-touching changes require security-reviewer at close.
notes:
plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-10-7lo-session-reset.md
verify: cd frontend && npm test

manual smoke (run after PR merge): log in as demo user (default fixture); navigate to /editor; observe stories list. Sign out → /login. Log in as a second user. Editor must show ONLY the second user's data (no stories from demo, no chapters, no chats). No browser refresh needed. Repeat the matrix: logout-then-login, sign-out-everywhere-then-login, delete-account-then-register-different.
close_reason: Closed

title: [Chat] Retry on 'ask' chat drops chapter+characters+worldNotes context (LLM acts blind)
description:
On a Chat (kind='ask') retry, the LLM receives no chapter content, no character context, and no world notes — it responds as if it only has the bare conversation history. Reproduces by clicking Regenerate on any Chat assistant message; the new reply will not match the chapter's voice/POV and may explicitly say "I don't see any previous parts of the story."

## Root cause

For action='ask', `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts:255-264` puts worldNotes + characters + chapterBlock + taskBlock into the synthesised USER message (not the system message). The system message contains only the generic system prompt.

`backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts:476-478` sends to Venice:
- normal turn: `[systemMsg, ...history, synthesisedUserMsg]` — context present
- retry turn: `[systemMsg, ...history]` — synthesisedUserMsg dropped

History is built from `priorMessagesForHistory.map(...)` at lines 447-472. Each user turn becomes `{role: 'user', content: m.contentJson}` (bare text). The 'ask' branch with attachment reframes as `User question: ...` but still doesn't include chapter/characters/worldNotes.

Result: on retry of an 'ask' chat, Venice receives no chapter context.

Scene chats are unaffected because action='scene' puts all context into the SYSTEM message (`prompt.service.ts:236-247`), which is preserved on retry.

## Why no test caught this

`backend/tests/routes/chat.test.ts` retry tests assert:
- `body.retry === true` reaches the wire (case C, case B)
- assistant count post-retry (1, not 2 — linear-replace)
- new content matches what Venice replied

None inspect the messages array sent to Venice for chapter/world/character content. An assertion like "body.messages.some(m => m.content.includes('Chapter so far'))" would fail today.

## Provenance

The synthesisedUserMsg-drop-on-retry was introduced by [SC6] (Scene tab) before PR #90 (AI Surfaces Unification). The [SC6] comment at `chat.routes.ts:473-475` reads "On retry the trailing user turn is already in history; do NOT append synthesisedUserMsg again or the model would see a duplicate user turn." That reasoning is correct for the BARE user text but misses that synthesisedUserMsg carries chapter context that the bare history version doesn't.

PR #90's Task 2 (deleteAllAfter wiring) re-touched this region but didn't change the message-array assembly; the bug pre-existed.

## Fix paths

(a) Filter `lastUserMsg.id` out of priorMessagesForHistory on retry, then use the unified `[systemMsg, ...history, synthesisedUserMsg]` for both branches. Smallest, cleanest change.

(b) Replace the lastUserMsg entry in history with the synthesised content (rebuild that entry via the same renderer the prompt uses).

(c) Move chapter/characters/worldNotes blocks into the SYSTEM message for action='ask' to match scene's structure. Larger blast radius (touches every ask call, not just retry) but eliminates this asymmetry permanently.

(a) is the smallest change and matches the existing logic shape. (c) is the architecturally cleaner long-term fix.

## Acceptance

- A retry of a Chat 'ask' message produces a Venice request whose user message contains "Chapter so far:" (when the chapter has content) and the same world-notes / characters blocks as the original turn.
- Add a backend test asserting the messages array on retry includes chapter context (specifically: an entry whose content includes "Chapter so far:").
- Existing case C / case B / linear-replace semantics remain correct.
- Scene retry remains unaffected.
notes:
verify: cd backend && npm test -- chat.test.ts

NOTE: do NOT take the stopgap fix path (a) — see story-editor-k1r for the architectural fix that closes this as a side effect. This issue stays open as the user-visible symptom; closes when k1r lands.
close_reason: Closed structurally by story-editor-k1r — chapter context now lives in systemMsg, retry preserves it by construction. Regression test at backend/tests/routes/chat.test.ts ([9ph] retry on ask preserves chapter context) asserts the invariant. Repo-boundary review CLEAN (ran for k1r on same diff).

title: [Frontend] Extract useStreamingAI primitive; consolidate Chat/Scene/AI-Completion streaming orchestration
description:
Three AI-streaming hooks each re-implement the same AbortController + apiStream + parseAiSseStream event-loop + error-mapping dance:

- Scene: streamMessage() in frontend/src/lib/api.ts:428 + useSceneTranscript.ts (imperative callbacks)
- Chat: useSendChatMessageMutation in frontend/src/hooks/useChat.ts:222 (TanStack mutation; abort wiring landed in bw2)
- AI Completion: useAICompletion.ts (bespoke hook with its own callback shape; used by Continue Writing + inline AI selection bubble)

The shared primitives (apiStream, parseAiSseStream) handle the wire format. What's duplicated three times is the orchestration above them: ~30 lines of AbortController + 'for await' event loop + done/chunk/error mapping + abort-signal-checked early returns. ~90 lines of structural copies.

The split is downstream-state-shape-driven, not protocol-driven:
- Scene has candidate cards (each assistant turn is an insertable card)
- Chat has the optimistic-bubble + cache-invalidation pattern (TanStack)
- AI Completion has inline TipTap editor decorations

The streaming machinery itself is identical across all three.

Maintenance debt is already visible: streamMessage's JSDoc has a 'TODO: expose an onCitations callback when a scene-tab caller needs it' comment. When the next AI feature lands, the implementer picks a flavour and copies a third implementation. This will only get worse as more AI features are added.

Goal: extract a useStreamingAI primitive (or stream-shaped utility — design decision) that owns AbortController + apiStream + parseAiSseStream loop + error mapping. Each consumer wraps it with its feature-specific state plumbing. Net: ~100-150 lines of deletion, one mental model for AI streaming in this app.

Acceptance:
- One shared streaming primitive with the abort + event-loop dance.
- Scene's streamMessage(), useSendChatMessageMutation, and useAICompletion all migrate to use it.
- All three downstream state shapes (candidate buffer, chat draft store, editor decorations) preserved unchanged.
- Existing tests for all three paths pass.
- streamMessage's onCitations TODO is resolved by the consolidation (in-flight citations available to all consumers if they want them).

Out of scope: changing any of the three downstream state shapes; backend changes; the SSE protocol itself.
design:
## Why three implementations exist (design history)

Each path was written when the protocol changed, and the implementer copied whichever sibling existed at the time (imperative vs TanStack vs custom hook). Nobody sat down to design the abstraction. The split is a fossil of incremental development, not an architectural decision.

## Per-event behaviour comparison

What each consumer does with each SSE event type:

| Step                | Scene                                          | Chat                                              | AI Completion                                       |
|---------------------|------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| Endpoint            | /chats/:id/messages                            | /chats/:id/messages                               | /ai/complete                                        |
| Body                | {content, retry, modelId}                      | adds attachment, enableWebSearch                  | totally different shape                             |
| Open + abort + loop | identical                                      | identical                                         | identical                                           |
| chunk event         | extract delta.content -> push to buffer        | extract delta.content -> push to bubble           | extract delta.content -> append to text             |
| done event          | finishAssistant(localId)                       | markDone() + invalidate query                     | flip status to 'done'                               |
| error event         | failAssistant(msg)                             | markError({code,msg}) + throw                     | flip status to 'error' + push to error store        |
| citations event     | ignored                                        | sent through to message cache                     | ignored                                             |
| Abort handling      | catch AbortError -> reset stream               | (gap — bw2 fixes)                                 | check signal.aborted -> early-return                |

Rows 3+ all differ only in WHICH callback the consumer plugs in. The streaming machinery itself is identical.

## Things that look different but aren't

- Different endpoints: that's a parameter, not divergence.
- Different body shapes: opaque to the streamer; pass through.
- Different state sinks: those are callbacks the consumer provides.
- Citations sometimes wanted: optional onCitations callback, default ignore.
- AI Completion harvests rate-limit headers: optional onResponseHeaders callback, or expose Response once.

## Things that ARE per-consumer (stay in the wrapper, not the primitive)

- Scene's pushUser -> beginStreamingAssistant -> finishAssistant transitions (candidate-card lifecycle).
- Chat's optimistic-bubble seed + cache invalidation on done.
- AI Completion's thinking -> streaming -> done state machine + tinted TipTap decoration.
- Re-entrant policies: AI Completion aborts in-flight before starting new (single-slot completion); Chat queues serially via TanStack mutation; Scene appends.

These are downstream concerns, not stream concerns. They belong in each wrapping hook, not in the streaming primitive.

## Suggested primitive shape (for the design pass)

```ts
function useStreamingAI<TBody>(opts: {
  endpoint: string;
  body: TBody;
  onChunk: (delta: string) => void;
  onCitations?: (citations: ...) => void;       // optional
  onResponseHeaders?: (res: Response) => void;  // optional, for rate-limit harvesting
  onDone: () => void;
  onError: (err: ApiError) => void;
}): { start: () => Promise<void>; stop: () => void; isStreaming: boolean }
```

Each consumer wraps it:
- Scene: hook callbacks drive useSceneTranscriptStore actions.
- Chat: TanStack mutation wraps start(); onChunk/onDone/onError drive useChatDraftStore.
- AI Completion: hook wraps start() with thinking->streaming->done state machine on local component state.

## Honest cost-benefit

Benefit of current split: zero. Each path duplicates the same dance.

Cost of consolidation: medium effort. All three call sites have integration tests; consolidating means re-running all three end-to-end flows. The chat draft store and the scene candidate model are non-trivial. Estimate: 1-2 days of focused work + review.

Cost of NOT consolidating: it gets worse as features land. The streamMessage onCitations TODO is the canary — Scene will eventually need in-flight citations and the choice will be (a) grow streamMessage to feature-parity with the mutation, or (b) move Scene to TanStack. Either way the duplication keeps pulling work.

title: bd-close-reviewed: handle already-closed bd issue gracefully
description:
scripts/bd-close-reviewed.sh --phase=close calls `bd close <id>` without checking whether the issue is already closed. If a controller invokes the skill twice (interrupted session, manual re-run, network blip), `bd close` may error or no-op depending on bd version.

Make it explicitly idempotent: check `bd show <id> --json` for status=closed first; exit 0 silently if already closed (with a one-line stdout note). The override-recording path (--override-block) should also short-circuit on already-closed (don't append another override line, don't create another empty trailer commit).

Verify is weak as filed (needs a sacrificial closed bd issue to exercise the dual-close path); tighten when implementing.
notes:
plan: trivial — small script change; defensive only
verify: bash scripts/test/bd-close-reviewed-idempotent.test.sh

title: [Editor] StoryPicker 'New story' is a no-op in the editor; 'Import .docx' looks broken (unwired handler)
description:
In the editor (after a story is selected on login), opening the Your Stories picker via the sidebar/header and clicking 'New story' or 'Import .docx' does nothing.

Root cause: EditorPage renders <StoryPicker> (frontend/src/pages/EditorPage.tsx:686-693) without onCreateStory or onImportDocx. StoryPicker forwards those props directly to the buttons' onClick (StoryPicker.tsx:151-156); when undefined, the Button primitive renders a native <button> with no handler — clicking is a no-op. The DashboardPage picker wires onCreateStory (opens a create StoryModal), which is why the same buttons work on the no-story landing surface but not in the editor.

Two sub-problems:
1. 'New story' — true regression: works on dashboard, dead in editor (EditorPage neither mounts StoryModal nor passes onCreateStory).
2. 'Import .docx' — never wired anywhere (not even dashboard); no backend import endpoint exists (StoryPicker JSDoc: 'TODO(future): no backend import endpoint yet. Render the button anyway.'). It's a no-op everywhere, not editor-specific.

Decided scope (user): fix 'New story' in the editor; stop 'Import .docx' from looking broken by hiding/disabling it wherever no handler is wired (no backend work). On create, navigate to the new story in BOTH the editor and the dashboard (dashboard currently does not navigate on create).

description:
StoryModal supports both mode='create' and mode='edit' (frontend/src/components/StoryModal.tsx:90), but the running app only mounts it in create mode (frontend/src/pages/DashboardPage.tsx:28-34). There is no affordance for the user to edit an existing story's title / description / worldNotes / includePreviousChaptersInPrompt — even though the backend PATCH /api/stories/:id supports all four and StoryModal's diffForPatch logic at lines 82-84 already builds the payload.

Surfaced 2026-05-25 in PR #118 review: the v6x manual test step 'toggle includePreviousChaptersInPrompt off via StoryModal' is unreachable for an existing story. A user who creates a story without unchecking the box can only flip it off by re-creating the story or by hitting the PATCH endpoint via devtools.

Scope:
1. Design (brainstorm + plan): pick an entry point. Candidates — gear icon in EditorPage sidebar header, row-hover edit pencil on StoryPicker, dedicated 'story settings' tab in EditorPage. Compare against the Character flow (EditorPage.tsx:138-148, 619) which already has the analogous pattern.
2. Implement: mount StoryModal mode='edit' from the chosen entry point; thread current story data as 'initial'; on save, optimistic update + invalidate the story query.
3. Update the v6x PR test step (or supersede with one against this issue) so the toggle path is actually exercisable.

Not blocking PR #118 — the backend + modal code are already shipped. This issue is for the missing UI mount.

title: Eliminate the shared-package dev watcher — backend consumes shared/src directly
description:
## Why

The `make dev` target spawns a host-side `tsc -p shared/tsconfig.build.json --watch` process (backgrounded, tracked via .watcher.pid, killed by `make stop` with a process-group kill). It exists solely because the backend resolves story-editor-shared through shared/package.json's "main": "./dist/index.js" — the *compiled* output — so shared-schema edits don't reach the running backend until shared/dist is rebuilt.

The frontend already sidesteps this: frontend/vite.config.ts:11 aliases story-editor-shared -> ../shared/src, so Vite compiles shared from source and needs no watcher.

The watcher is a host process in an otherwise fully-containerized dev flow, and its .watcher.pid lifecycle is fragile (it needs healthy host node_modules to run tsc — the very thing the story-editor-lki permissions bug breaks). Surfaced while reviewing the story-editor-lki dev-container-permissions plan.

## What

Make the backend consume shared/src directly in dev, the way the frontend already does, so ts-node-dev --transpile-only transpiles shared's source in-memory and the watcher can be deleted.

- Add a 'development' condition to shared/package.json's exports map pointing at ./src/index.ts (or a backend tsconfig.json paths mapping for story-editor-shared -> ../shared/src/index.ts), gated so it only applies to the dev runtime.
- Keep the prod path unchanged: backend/Dockerfile builder still runs npm -w story-editor-shared run build, and the prod runtime still resolves story-editor-shared -> shared/dist/index.js via the unchanged "main".
- Remove the watcher from the dev Makefile target, the .watcher.pid handling from stop, and the build-time npm -w story-editor-shared run build from the backend dev Dockerfile stage.

## Design notes

- shared/package.json today: "main": "./dist/index.js", "types": "./src/index.ts" (types already point at src — only the runtime entry is compiled).
- Backend dev: ts-node-dev --respawn --transpile-only src/index.ts. --transpile-only means no typecheck on shared's source — consistent with how backend runs its own code; npm run typecheck still covers it separately.
- The conditional-exports approach needs the dev runtime invoked with the 'development' condition resolved (Node --conditions=development, or whatever ts-node-dev honours) — confirm ts-node-dev's resolution behaviour during the plan. tsconfig paths + tsconfig-paths/register is the fallback if exports conditions don't resolve cleanly under ts-node-dev.
- Backend tsconfig.json has rootDir: src — a real tsc reading ../shared/src would trip rootDir, but backend dev uses --transpile-only and prod uses the unchanged main: dist, so the real tsc build path must be confirmed untouched.
- Mirror the existing precedent: frontend/vite.config.ts:11.

## Sequencing

Touches the backend/Dockerfile dev stage, which story-editor-lki also rewrites — depends on story-editor-lki to avoid a merge conflict. Not a logical blocker otherwise.
acceptance_criteria: make dev spawns no host-side tsc --watch and creates no .watcher.pid; make stop no longer references .watcher.pid; editing a shared/src/*.ts file is picked up by the backend dev server with no manual shared build; backend dev Dockerfile stage no longer runs npm -w story-editor-shared run build; prod build path unchanged (shared/dist still built and resolved in the runner); npm -w story-editor-backend run build and typecheck still pass; backend test suite still passes.

title: Extract narrative-entity ownership predicates into the repo layer (close requireOwnership Prisma bypass)
description:
## Why

`backend/src/middleware/ownership.middleware.ts` reads all six narrative tables (Story, Chapter, Character, OutlineItem, Chat, Message) via Prisma directly — bypassing the repo layer. `checkOwned` hand-rolls the ownership-graph `where` chains (`{ id, chat: { chapter: { story: { userId } } } }` etc.).

Low-risk *today* — it selects only `{ id: true }`, so no ciphertext or plaintext narrative content can leak — but worth closing:

1. The ownership-graph `where` chains ARE the app's authorization logic, and they live in the least-governed place: hand-rolled, duplicated against the repos + Prisma schema relations, with nothing structurally forcing them to stay correct. A wrong chain is a silent horizontal-privilege-escalation bug.
2. It's a cited precedent for "middleware may reach past the repo for narrative tables if it only selects scalars" — which drifts toward `{ id: true, title: true }` and a real leak.
3. It can't inherit future repo-level invariants (soft-delete, audit, extra scoping).

Surfaced by repo-boundary-reviewer during the story-editor-d7e close-gate as a NON_BLOCKING finding.

## What

1. Add a module-level ownership predicate per narrative repo — `storyExistsForUser(id, userId): Promise<boolean>` in story.repo.ts, plus `chapterExistsForUser`, `characterExistsForUser`, `outlineItemExistsForUser`, `chatExistsForUser`, `messageExistsForUser`. Each owns its own ownership-chain `where` clause.
2. Collapse `checkOwned` in ownership.middleware.ts to a dispatch table `OwnedResource -> predicate`. No per-entity Prisma left in the middleware.
3. Add a test that enumerates every `OwnedResource`: seed an owned + a not-owned row of each type, assert `requireOwnership` 403s the not-owned case and calls next() on the owned case. This governs the ownership chains so a future drift fails CI.

## Out of scope

- The request-scoped `create*Repo(req)` factories and their encrypt/decrypt paths — unchanged.
- The 403-conflation behaviour (not-found vs not-owned stay the same 403) — keep as-is.
design: Predicates are module-level exports, NOT methods on the request-scoped `create*Repo(req)` factory: an ownership check is a pure existence predicate that needs no DEK, so threading `req` through just to reach a DEK-free method would be ceremony. They live in the same file as the entity's repo so the ownership-chain knowledge is co-located with the entity that owns it — a future re-parenting change lands in the obvious place.
acceptance_criteria:
- ownership.middleware.ts contains no `client.<model>.findFirst` calls; checkOwned is a dispatch table over OwnedResource.
- Each of the 6 narrative repos exports a `*ExistsForUser(id, userId)` predicate carrying its own ownership-chain where clause.
- New enumeration test in tests/middleware/ exercises all 6 OwnedResource types (owned + not-owned each) and passes.
- backend typecheck + full backend test suite green; encryption-leak test still passes.
notes: verify: npm -w story-editor-backend run typecheck && npm -w story-editor-backend test -- tests/middleware/ownership tests/repos tests/security/encryption-leak

title: Bring backend/tests + frontend/tests into the typecheck gate
description:
The Message consolidation review (PR #104) surfaced a structural gap: both backend/tsconfig.json and frontend/tsconfig.app.json exclude tests/, so test files are not part of \`tsc --noEmit\` runs. Vitest transpiles them at run time, so they execute, but type drift between fixtures and source isn't caught by the typecheck gate.

Concrete example caught during PR review: backend/tests/security/encryption-leak.test.ts:132 used \`attachmentJson: { ref: ... }\` after the rename, but MessageCreateInput.attachmentJson is typed \`MessageAttachment | null\` where MessageAttachment requires { selectionText, chapterId }. The test only compiled because tests/ is outside the type gate. It was found by manual review, not by CI.

## Acceptance

- Add a tsconfig.test.json (or extend existing tsconfig to include tests/) for both backend and frontend.
- Wire it into the typecheck script (or add a second \`typecheck:tests\` script that gets run alongside \`typecheck\` in CI and pre-commit hooks).
- Confirm at least one fixture-drift case is now caught: e.g. revert backend/tests/security/encryption-leak.test.ts:132 to the broken shape and verify the new gate fails.

## Why this matters

The consolidation work (j76 + the four other narrative-entity migrations still pending in bd) leans heavily on the type gate to prevent drift between shared schemas and consumer code. Tests are full-fledged consumers of those types — they just live outside the gate today. Closing the gap is a small one-off, but the silent-harm path (fixtures rotting against canonical types) grows with every future entity migration.

## Discovered during

PR #104 review (Message entity consolidation). Same review caught a content-type mismatch that the type gate would have prevented if tests had been in scope.

plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-14-tests-in-typecheck-gate.md

close_reason: Brought backend/tests + frontend/tests into the typecheck gate. Backend: corrected tsconfig.test.json (exclude override + @/* paths) + fixed 29 fixture-drift errors + wired. Frontend: tsconfig.test.json + fixed 206 errors across 5 clusters + wired. Both gates pass; 1054 frontend tests + full backend suite green; regression proof confirmed.

- Are there other CLAUDE.md "General" rules that belong in general.md but didn't make the first cut?

- Is the bridge-skill behaviour (union of matched digests) still correct, or do we need to special-case general.md as "always prepended"?

The original digest fix is mechanical and won't change in scope. The "did we miss anything" question needs a few dispatches of real-world experience to answer — premature to bundle.

title: Investigate Docker frontend/node_modules root-ownership flip
description: During the Character consolidation close-gate verify, frontend/node_modules/.vite-temp was root-owned, blocking vitest with EACCES. The dev container bind-mounts ./frontend:/app/frontend and runs as root by default — the anonymous volume on /app/node_modules protects the hoisted root tree, but anything Vite writes under /app/frontend/node_modules/ from the container lands as root on the host. Either run the dev container as a non-root user (uid 1000) or document the cleanup step in SELF_HOSTING.md.

title: [Auth] 'Session expired' banner flashes on explicit logout when access token is already invalid
description:
## Bug

When a user clicks Sign Out and their access token has already expired server-side, the LoginPage briefly (or persistently, depending on timing) renders the banner 'Your session has expired. Sign in again to continue.' even though the logout was explicit, not a session expiry.

## Trace

1. `useAuth.logout()` fires `await api('/auth/logout', { method: 'POST' })`.
2. /auth/logout returns 401 (token already expired server-side).
3. api client tries refreshAccessToken() — fails (refresh cookie also expired/wonky).
4. api client invokes `onUnauthorized()` → `handleUnauthorizedAccess()` (frontend/src/store/session.ts:74-83) → `clearSession({ expired: true })` → sets sessionExpired=true, status='unauthenticated'.
5. RequireAuth redirects to /login. LoginPage reads sessionExpired=true → 'Your session has expired' banner renders.
6. Meanwhile, the api call's thrown ApiError propagates back to `useAuth.logout`. Catch ignores. Finally runs `await resetClientState(qc); clearSession();` — clearSession() (no args) sets sessionExpired=false.
7. The `await resetClientState` in finally yields the microtask. During the yield, LoginPage has already mounted with the banner. After the await resolves and clearSession() runs, LoginPage re-renders without the banner.

Net effect: user sees a 'session expired' banner flash (or persist, if the await is slow) on every explicit logout where the token expired server-side.

## Why this surfaced now

7lo introduced clearSession({ expired }) and the registered-QC variant. The race existed before in a different shape (handleUnauthorizedAccess directly mutated sessionExpired and status), but 7lo's atomic clearSession({ expired: true }) made the timing more visible because the await in finally now sits between the unauthorized-handler firing and the corrective clearSession().

The user reported this immediately after 7lo merged.

## Reproduction

1. Log in successfully.
2. Wait for access token to expire OR manually invalidate it (server-side or by editing the cookie).
3. Click 'Sign out'.
4. Expected: clean redirect to /login with no banner.
5. Actual: 'Your session has expired' banner appears (briefly or persistently).

## Fix sketches (pick one during plan)

**A. Per-request skipUnauthorizedHandler flag in api.ts.** Add an opt-in field on ApiRequestInit; `useAuth.logout` and `useDeleteAccountMutation` (and possibly `useSignOutEverywhereMutation`) pass it. Cleanest separation; obvious at call sites.

**B. Bare fetch for /auth/logout.** logout() doesn't actually need refresh-retry semantics — a 401 is the same outcome as success. Use a raw fetch, no auth retry, no unauthorized handler. Less code; more intention-revealing; but harder to discover.

**C. setSessionExpired(false) synchronously at the top of logout.** Doesn't fix the race; just narrows the window. Reject.

**D. Treat 401 on /auth/logout as a no-op in api.ts itself.** Path-conditional behavior. Coupling concern; reject.

Approach A is cleanest. Approach B is more idiomatic. Either fix is small (one helper, one call site).

## Acceptance

- After explicit Sign Out (logout, sign-out-everywhere, delete-account), the LoginPage banner is determined by location.state ONLY — sessionExpired is never read or written during explicit-logout flows.
- Test: simulate logout where /auth/logout returns 401 and refresh returns 401. Assert: no 'session expired' banner shown on the resulting LoginPage.

## Related

- story-editor-7lo (parent — this race surfaced after the 7lo refactor; same auth-transition surface).

title: [Auth] Cross-account leak: in-flight AI / chat SSE streams not aborted on session transition
description:
Sister bug to story-editor-7lo. The session-reset helper (`resetClientState`) in 7lo cancels TanStack Query fetches via `queryClient.cancelQueries()`, but **does not** abort in-flight SSE streams for AI completions or chat messages. If a user logs out (or sign-out-everywhere / delete-account / terminal-401) mid-stream, the previous user's stream keeps appending tokens into the freshly-reset client state, briefly rendering plaintext narrative content under the new session.

## Root cause

Each streaming surface owns its own `AbortController` at the mutation level:

- `frontend/src/hooks/useChat.ts:231` — `abortRef = useRef<AbortController | null>(null)`. The mutation creates a fresh controller per send, plumbs `controller.signal` into `runStreamingAI`. The controller is reachable only from inside the mutation closure; nothing outside the mutation can abort it.
- `frontend/src/hooks/useAICompletion.ts:72` — same shape, separate controllerRef.

`resetClientState(qc)` calls `qc.cancelQueries()`, which cancels TanStack Query fetches but **not** these mutation-owned streams (mutations are not queries; their AbortControllers are not registered with the QueryClient).

## Reproduction

1. Log in as user A.
2. Open a chat with a long-form scene assistant prompt; click Send so the assistant streaming response is in flight.
3. While tokens are still streaming into the chat draft (assistant text bubble visibly growing), open the user menu and click Sign out.
4. Land on /login. Log in as user B.
5. **Bug:** for ~milliseconds-to-seconds (depending on remaining stream length), B's newly-mounted chat draft store may briefly render A's remaining assistant tokens before the next reset (if any) clears them.

The same shape applies to the inline AI completion surface (`useAICompletion`): rewrite/expand/describe/ask streams keep flowing into `inlineAIResult` after a session swap.

## Why 7lo doesn't close this

7lo's `resetClientState` helper hits TanStack Query + Zustand stores. It treats Zustand as the only push surface. SSE streams push directly into `chatDraft.appendDelta` and (via different plumbing) into `inlineAIResult.text`. The helper's reset wins the race against ONE chunk; the next chunk arrives and re-populates the store.

## Fix sketch (for the bd issue, not a plan)

Two reasonable shapes:

1. **App-level AbortController registry**, mirroring 7lo's `registerSessionResetQueryClient` pattern. Each streaming surface registers its controller on creation, deregisters on completion. `resetClientState` calls `abortAllStreams()` alongside `cancelQueries()`.
2. **Subscribe to session changes from the streaming hook**: when `useSessionStore`'s `user.id` changes (or `status` flips to `unauthenticated`), the hook aborts its current controller. Localizes the rule to the streaming sites; doesn't require a global registry.

Approach 2 is more idiomatic 2026 (identity-scoped state by construction). Approach 1 is faster to ship and matches the registry pattern 7lo establishes. Pick during plan.

## Acceptance

- After logout/sign-out-everywhere/delete-account/terminal-401 mid-stream, no further chunks land in `chatDraft.drafts[*]` or `inlineAIResult` after the reset runs.
- Test: drive the chat send mutation, simulate a logout while the stream is open, assert the AbortController has been aborted AND no further `appendDelta` calls occur after reset.

## Sequencing

After story-editor-7lo merges. (7lo establishes the helper this issue extends; landing this first would create avoidable conflict.)

## Related

- story-editor-7lo (parent leak fix, this PR's sister).
- The 2026-direction follow-ups noted in 7lo's plan: queryKey-by-userId, UserScope subtree, BroadcastChannel multi-tab. Approach 2 above is a stepping-stone toward UserScope-shaped state.
notes:
plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-02-abort-streams-on-session-transition.md
verify: cd frontend && npm test
blocked-by: story-editor-7lo merging first
close_reason: Abort in-flight chat + inline-AI SSE streams on every session transition via a stream-controller registry wired into resetClientState. Closes the cross-account leak where the previous user's decrypted tokens could land in the next user's freshly-reset state.

title: [Backend] Unify prompt-building: one canonical message-array shape across all actions
description:
The retry-loses-chapter-context bug (story-editor-9ph) surfaced a deeper asymmetry: `buildPrompt` in `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts` produces structurally different message arrays per action, and that asymmetry leaks into other parts of the system that assume a uniform shape (most visibly: the chat retry path drops the synthesised user message, which silently drops chapter context for `ask` but not `scene`).

Goal: find one common method for building the different prompts across all parts of the app, with the message-array shape uniform across actions. Don't ship a stopgap on 9ph that perpetuates the divergence — fix the underlying construction instead.

## Current asymmetry

`prompt.service.ts:174-269` — single `buildPrompt(input)` function with two structurally different return paths:

**`scene` action** (lines 232-251): all context blocks live in the system message; user message is the bare freeformInstruction.

```
system:  systemContent + worldNotesBlock + charactersBlock + chapterBlock + sceneTemplate
user:    freeformInstruction
```

**All other actions** — `ask`, `continue`, `rephrase`/`rewrite`, `expand`, `summarise`, `describe`, `freeform` (lines 253-269): context blocks live in the user message; system message is just the generic prompt.

```
system:  systemContent
user:    worldNotesBlock + charactersBlock + chapterBlock + taskBlock
```

This bifurcation drove story-editor-9ph: the chat retry path strips the synthesised user message (because the trailing user is already in history), which incidentally strips chapter context for `ask` but not for `scene`.

## Other consequences of the split

- Token-budget calculation in `buildPrompt` has to special-case scene (`fixedTokens` includes `sceneTemplate` instead of `taskBlock`).
- The `ask` history-mapping in `chat.routes.ts:455-466` needs a special branch for prior turns with attachments (re-rendering via `renderAskUserContent`) because the bare-text version in history doesn't match the synthesised version that originally went to Venice. Scene history doesn't need this branch.
- Future actions (e.g. retry-with-instruction, edit-then-regenerate, multi-turn scene continuation) will face the same fork: which action-flavor do they belong to, and where does context go?
- Test surface is doubled: every prompt-shape test has to think about both layouts.

## Proposed direction

Move toward a single canonical message-array layout for all actions, with chapter / characters / world-notes in the **system message** (matches `scene`'s shape, since scene has been the reference for "the model has full context"). The user message becomes the action-specific instruction only.

Concretely:

1. Refactor `buildPrompt` so every action assembles the system message from the same building blocks (`systemContent`, `worldNotesBlock`, `charactersBlock`, `chapterBlock`, `taskBlock` — where `taskBlock` is the action-specific instruction, currently it's the user content).
2. The user message becomes the user's literal input (chat: their question; scene: their direction; inline AI: the selection text + freeform if any).
3. Update token budget to compute `fixedTokens` once across all actions (no scene-vs-other branch).
4. Update `chat.routes.ts` history-mapping to drop the `ask` attachment-rewrap branch (no longer needed — the synthesised content matches the bare history entry by construction).
5. Update `chat.routes.ts:476-478` to use a single `[systemMsg, ...history, userMsg]` form for both retry and non-retry; on retry, exclude the trailing user from history because it's now equivalent to userMsg.

## Wider scope: prompt-building convergence across the app

Beyond `chat.routes.ts` + `prompt.service.ts`, audit:

- `backend/src/routes/ai.routes.ts` (inline AI completion) — uses the same `buildPrompt` but with action `continue`/`rephrase`/etc. Does its message-array assembly differ from chat? If so, normalize.
- Any other prompt-building entry points (search for `buildPrompt(` and `messages: [...` in backend/src). Goal: one renderer, one shape, one set of tests.
- Frontend has no prompt builder (Venice calls are server-side only) — but the frontend-side row/render layer should keep matching the server's notion of "user said X" vs "the system framed X." Right now, what the user typed and what reaches Venice as a `user` message can be very different (e.g. for `ask`, "Can you continue?" becomes a 2KB message with chapter content prepended). Should the user-facing transcript ever expose what was actually sent? Out of scope for this issue, but flag.

## Why not stop-gap path (a) on 9ph

Path (a) on 9ph — filtering `lastUserMsg.id` out of `priorMessagesForHistory` on retry, then unifying the messages array — fixes the immediate symptom but bakes the asymmetry deeper into the route logic. It's another special-case for `ask`'s structural quirk. Don't ship it; fix the root cause.

## Acceptance

- One canonical message-array shape across all `buildPrompt` actions.
- Chat 'ask' retry preserves chapter context (closes 9ph as a side-effect).
- All existing prompt tests pass; new tests added for the unified shape.
- L-series live test (or staging smoke) confirms Venice produces equivalent outputs on the new shape (i.e. the move from user-message-context to system-message-context for `ask` doesn't measurably degrade quality — sample some Chat replies before/after).
- No `if (action === 'scene')` or `if (action === 'ask')` branches remain in `buildPrompt` outside the `taskBlock` itself.
- `chat.routes.ts:455-466` ask-attachment-rewrap branch can be removed.
- Doc the layout decision in `docs/agent-rules/backend.md` (AI section) so future actions inherit it.

## Cross-links

- Closes (or supersedes): story-editor-9ph (Chat retry context drop — same bug, deeper fix).
- Touches: `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts`, `backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts`, `backend/src/routes/ai.routes.ts`, `backend/tests/services/prompt.service.test.ts` (or wherever prompt tests live), `backend/tests/routes/chat.test.ts`.
- Spec: needs a brainstorm + plan before implementation. The shape change has L-series implications.

title: [Chat/Scene] Tab session default selection picks oldest session, should pick most-recent
description:
When entering a Chat or Scene tab (or switching between them) for a chapter that already has prior sessions, the tab defaults to the first/oldest session in the list instead of the most recently-touched one.

Locations:
- frontend/src/components/ChatTab.tsx — the useEffect that runs setActiveChatId(sessions[0].id) when activeChatId === null && sessions.length > 0.
- frontend/src/components/SceneTab.tsx — same pattern.

Fix paths:
  (a) Sort the sessions query result by updatedAt desc on the server side (in useChatsQuery / its backend endpoint), so sessions[0] is the right pick by construction.
  (b) Sort client-side in the default-select effect: pick sessions reduce-max by updatedAt instead of [0].
  (c) Persist last-active session per (chapterId, kind) in user settings or local storage and restore on tab open.

(a) is cleanest if no other consumer relies on a stable order. (c) is what most chat UIs do but adds state. (b) is the smallest fix.

Session-picker dropdown shows the order independently — verify whether its current order also needs adjusting for consistency.

Reproduction: open a chapter with at least 2 chat sessions, click the most recent one, then leave the chapter and come back (or switch to Scene tab and back to Chat) — Chat tab will land on the oldest session instead of the one the user just used.

title: [Chat] Per-message Regenerate button is not wired — onRegenerateMessage prop never passed
description:
The Regenerate (Retry) icon button shown next to each assistant message in the Chat transcript does nothing when clicked. Confirmed by reading the wiring:

## Trace

`frontend/src/components/ChatMessages.tsx:40` declares the prop on `ChatMessagesProps`:
```ts
onRegenerateMessage?: (id: string) => void;
```

`ChatMessages.tsx:201-202` defines the click handler:
```ts
const handleRegenerate = (): void => {
  if (onRegenerate) onRegenerate(message.id);
};
```

— this is a guarded call, so when `onRegenerate` is undefined, the click is a silent no-op. The button still renders (lines 227-229: `aria-label="Regenerate"`, `title="Regenerate"`, `onClick={handleRegenerate}`).

`ChatTab.tsx:178-182` mounts `ChatMessages` with only **three** props:
```tsx
<ChatMessages
  chatId={activeChatId}
  sendError={sendChatMessage.error}
  onRetrySend={onRetry}
/>
```

The `onRegenerateMessage` prop is **never passed**, so `onRegenerate` is undefined inside `AssistantMessage`, so the button is dead.

(`onRetrySend` is a different code path — it's wired to the `InlineErrorBanner` for after-an-error retries, not per-message regenerate.)

## What "regenerate" should do

A user clicks Regenerate on the trailing assistant message → the chat replays that turn's user message through Venice with the same model + same context, replacing the assistant message in place (or appending a new candidate, depending on UX choice — see story-editor-a9v which is grappling with the same question for Scene's "candidate" model). The `useSendChatMessageMutation` already accepts a `retry: true` flag (`backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts` accepts `retry: true` in the body, and `useChat.ts:208-210` types `retry?: boolean`) — the wiring just needs:

1. A handler in `ChatTab` that calls `sendChatMessage.mutate({ chatId, modelId, retry: true })` (no new user content).
2. Pass that handler as `onRegenerateMessage` on the `<ChatMessages>` JSX.

## Open question (might fold into story-editor-a9v)

Per-message regenerate has a UX choice: replace the existing assistant turn, or append a new candidate (Scene-style)? The post-`x29 retry` recent commit `954b1ef [scene-tab] fix: render every assistant message as its own candidate card (retry visible)` settled this for Scene as "append a new candidate." Chat should pick the same model — and that's exactly the question story-editor-a9v is asking. **This bug fix should probably wait for a9v's design pass** so the wired-up Retry behaves consistently with whatever a9v decides; OR, ship a stop-gap "replace trailing assistant message" wiring now and revisit when a9v lands.

## Acceptance

- Clicking Regenerate on an assistant message triggers a Venice round-trip that replays the matching user turn with the same model + context.
- Behaviour aligns with whatever story-editor-a9v decides (replace-in-place vs append-new-candidate). If a9v hasn't landed when this is picked up, file a `block:` link on a9v.
- Tests exercise the click → mutation path. Add a `<ChatTab>` test with a stubbed `apiStream` mocking the SSE response and asserting the second `apiStream` call has `retry: true` in its body.

Touches: `ChatTab.tsx` (handler + prop wiring), `ChatMessages.tsx` (no behavioural change unless a9v decides to add a candidate-list visual), `useChat.ts` (no API change — the `retry` flag is already supported), tests.

title: Settings → Models: Reset to defaults is a server-side no-op
description:
Reset to defaults visibly resets the sliders for one frame, then the values revert. The backend silently keeps the old override values.

Root cause (in backend/src/routes/user-settings.routes.ts via backend/src/lib/deep-merge.ts):

The reset handler in frontend/src/components/SettingsModelsTab.tsx sends:
  PATCH /api/users/me/settings { chat: { overrides: { ...prev, [activeModelId]: {} } } }

The backend persists `deepMerge(stored, patch)`. deepMerge recurses into chat.overrides[modelId]: target is `{ temperature: ..., topP: ... }` (plain object), source is `{}` (plain object), so it recurses again — and a deepMerge with an empty source returns the target unchanged. So the empty entry is a no-op on the server; old override values stay persisted.

Symptom timeline matches:
1. Frontend mergeSettings does shallow merge on `chat`, so the optimistic update replaces chat.overrides wholesale → sliders flash to defaults.
2. PATCH response arrives with the unchanged-server-state → cache reverts → sliders snap back.

Fix: in the PATCH handler, treat each entry in chat.overrides as atomic — replace per-modelId rather than deep-merging into the entry. An empty `{}` then correctly clears that model overrides. The rest of the settings tree continues to deep-merge as before.

Test the fix at the route level: send { chat: { overrides: { m1: {} } } } after a prior PATCH that set { m1: { temperature: 1.5 } }, assert response shows overrides.m1 = {}.

Other models in chat.overrides must not be affected by a single-model reset.

title: Consolidate inline Character fixtures in Storybook stories onto a shared typed factory
description:
Discovered during story-editor-ote (test fixture factory consolidation). The acceptance demo for ote — temporarily adding a required field to characterSchema — surfaced 7 compile errors in production Storybook stories that carry their own inline Character object literals, independent of the test suite:

- frontend/src/components/CastTab.stories.tsx (5 inline Character literals: ~lines 11, 29, 47, 65, 83)
- frontend/src/components/CharacterSheet.stories.tsx (2 inline Character literals: ~lines 13, 32)

These are the same drift-amplification problem ote fixed for the test suite, but for Storybook: one Character schema field add = 7 simultaneous edits across story files. They were OUT OF SCOPE for ote (which targeted frontend/tests/ fixtures only), so they were left untouched by design.

## Proposal
- ote created typed factories under frontend/tests/fixtures/ (makeCharacter etc.). Stories live in src/ and (per Vite config) cannot import from tests/. Decide the shared location during brainstorming: either a src-visible fixtures module (e.g. frontend/src/test-support/ or a stories-local helper) or accept a small amount of duplication. This is a design decision, not a mechanical port.
- Replace the 7 inline Character literals with calls to the chosen shared factory so a future schema field add fails at one place.
- Pure Storybook/story refactor: no component behavior change, no test count change.

## Why a separate issue
Crossing the tests/ ↔ src/ import boundary needs a design decision (where the shared story fixture lives), so it is not a trivial port of the ote test factory. Filed clean rather than scope-creeping ote.

## Acceptance
- The 7 inline Character literals in CastTab.stories.tsx + CharacterSheet.stories.tsx replaced by a shared typed factory call.
- Adding a hypothetical required Character field produces ONE story-side compile error (the factory), demonstrable as a quick check.
- npm --prefix frontend run typecheck stays green; Storybook still builds.
notes: verify: npm --prefix frontend run typecheck && npm --prefix frontend run build

title: Consolidate duplicated test fixture factories (Character/Chapter/Model) into typed shared helpers
description:
Discovered during story-editor-4ry (tests-in-typecheck-gate). The frontend test suite has ~6 independent makeCharacter-style fixture factories across separate test files (CastTab.test.tsx, CastTab.delete.test.tsx, CastTab.dragA11y.test.tsx, CharRefSuggestion.test.tsx, CharacterPopover.test.tsx, CharacterPopoverHost.test.tsx), plus similar duplication for Chapter (ChapterList.dragA11y, ChapterReorder) and Model (ChatComposer) fixtures.

When the Character schema gained backstory/relationships/color/initial, all six Character factories needed the identical edit. The typecheck gate (now wired by story-editor-4ry) CATCHES this drift, but the amplification — one schema field add = N simultaneous fixture edits — is exactly the maintainability cost this kind of gate is meant to reduce.

## Proposal
- Add typed fixture factories under frontend/tests/fixtures/ (e.g. character.ts, chapter.ts, model.ts), each with an explicit return type annotation (: Character / : ChapterMeta / : Model) so a future schema addition fails at the single factory, not at N call sites.
- Migrate the duplicated per-file helpers to import the shared factory (allowing per-call overrides via a Partial<> param, matching the existing helper ergonomics).
- Pure test refactor: no production code, no behavior change, test count unchanged.

## Why now / why not in 4ry
Consolidating mid-task in 4ry would have been scope creep (the task was a type-error burndown). Filed as a clean follow-up. Code-quality reviewer flagged it as the one structural finding worth a follow-up.

## Acceptance
- One typed factory per entity under frontend/tests/fixtures/; the ~6 Character + Chapter + Model per-file helpers replaced by imports of it.
- frontend typecheck gate stays green; full frontend suite passes at the same test count.
- Adding a hypothetical required field to the Character schema produces exactly ONE fixture compile error (the factory), demonstrable as a quick check.

title: Unify error display: inline-banner vs. DevErrorOverlay toast surfaces
description:
Today there are two error-display surfaces with overlapping coverage and no clear contract for which to use:

1. **Inline banner** (`VeniceErrorBanner` wrapping `InlineErrorBanner`) — rendered per-feature in `InlineAIResult`, `ContinueWriting`, `TranscriptView` send-error branch. Per-code affordances (countdown, Open Settings, Top up, veniceMessage echo). Local to the feature that caused the error.

2. **DevErrorOverlay toast** (`frontend/src/components/DevErrorOverlay.tsx`) — bottom-right strip in production, full stack in debug mode. Reads from `useErrorStore` (`frontend/src/store/errors.ts`). Globally mounted.

Surveyed by story-editor-c0c (Venice error passthrough) closeout. Push sites today:

| Push site | What pushes | Has inline banner? |
|---|---|---|
| useAICompletion.ts:124 | Every /api/ai/complete failure | **Yes — fully redundant.** Both InlineAIResult/ContinueWriting render VeniceErrorBanner AND the toast appears. |
| SceneTab.tsx:102,164 | checkChatSendGuards rejections (no_chapter, no_model) | No — guards fire before mutation, so TranscriptView.sendError is null. Toast is the only surface. |
| ChatTab.tsx:83,185 | Same checkChatSendGuards pattern | No — same reason. |
| useUserSettings.ts:213 | Settings save (patch) failures | No — Settings modal has no inline error region for this. |
| Settings.tsx:218 | Venice-key save errors | Partial — Settings.tsx has venice-key-save-error testid for inline display; this toast push might be redundant. |

**Decisions needed:**
1. **Which surface owns which class of error?** Proposed: feature-local mutations + AI calls → inline banner; cross-cutting concerns (network down, terminal-401, schema mismatch) → toast. Guard rejections (no_chapter, no_model) are ambiguous — they fire before the mutation, so feature-local inline banner has no shape to consume; either give the inline surface a guard-error mode or keep these on the toast with a clear convention.
2. **Drop the redundant useAICompletion.ts:124 push** (the only fully-redundant one — VeniceErrorBanner already covers it end-to-end).
3. **Audit Settings.tsx:218** — does the venice-key-save-error inline path fully cover the toast push? If yes, drop the push.
4. **Decide on DevErrorOverlay's debug-mode behavior.** In debug mode it shows the full error stack, useful for development. Even if we drop most prod pushes, debug-mode visibility for AI errors might still be valuable — needs a call.

**Why this exists:** Surfaced 2026-05-17 during c0c closeout. The dual-surface pattern grew organically — each feature added its own inline banner without revisiting the existing toast pushes. Without a contract, future contributors will copy whichever pattern they see first, deepening the inconsistency.

verify: design decision per piece — no single automated verify; the fix may include a docs/agent-rules entry naming the contract and a test that asserts the redundant-push call is gone.
notes:
related: story-editor-c0c (Venice error passthrough — closed 2026-05-17; introduced VeniceErrorBanner across all three AI surfaces, which created the redundant-toast smell)
verify: design decision per piece — no single automated verify

title: Richer Venice error passthrough: codes, retry-after, upstream status to UI
description:
Today most Venice errors get flattened to generic 500/internal_error envelopes by the time they reach the frontend. The Venice client wrapper (backend/src/lib/venice-errors.ts) has mapVeniceError / mapVeniceErrorToSse but the taxonomy is thin and the surface area for richer passthrough is small.

Concretely, the user-visible improvements: distinguish 'Venice rate-limited (try again in N seconds)' from 'Venice auth failure (check your BYOK key)' from 'Venice upstream timeout' in both the AI completion path and the chat send path. Today most of these collapse into a generic error toast.

## Pieces (likely one PR per piece, but file as a single task and split if scope balloons)

1. **Backend: expand venice-errors.ts with granular subclasses.** Distinguish:
   - VeniceRateLimitError (429, with retryAfterSeconds from Retry-After header)
   - VeniceUnauthorizedError (401 — bad/expired BYOK key)
   - VeniceQuotaExhaustedError (402-ish — billing/quota exceeded; check actual Venice code)
   - VeniceUpstreamUnavailableError (503/504/timeout)
   - VeniceModelUnavailableError (the model returned 4xx for model-not-found)
   - Keep the existing generic VeniceError as the base
2. **Backend: error-code taxonomy.** Document the stable error codes the frontend can switch on: 'venice_rate_limited', 'venice_unauthorized', 'venice_quota_exhausted', 'venice_upstream_unavailable', 'venice_model_unavailable', 'venice_unknown'. Put the catalog in docs/api-contract.md or a sibling.
3. **Backend: route catches that map Venice subclasses.** In ai.routes.ts and chat.routes.ts (the two routes that call Venice), the try/catch around the Venice call maps each subclass to the right HTTP shape + code. The validateBody PR (story-editor-xgb) drops boilerplate catches but preserves substantive ones — this work fits in those preserved catches.
4. **Frontend: extend useAICompletion + chat send error state.** Carry retryAfterSeconds, upstreamStatus, and the code. The shared error type from story-editor-shared should cover all the codes from step 2.
5. **Frontend: UI rendering.** Surface 'try again in 23s' on rate-limit, 'check your Venice API key in Settings' on auth, etc. Reuse the rate-limit indicator from story-editor-p75 once that ships.

## Why this exists
Surfaced during the validateBody/xgb brainstorm (2026-05-16). The xgb PR makes the per-route catches that DO Venice error mapping stand out; this is the natural follow-up to make those catches do meaningful mapping rather than just forwarding. Currently blocked on no one bothering to write the code — there's no dependency gate beyond xgb landing (and even that's not strict — this work can be done independently).

verify: design decision per piece — no single automated verify; each sub-PR adds its own tests

description:
The 'count words in a trimmed text' rule lives in three places that must agree but have no compile-time link:

description: The Chapter entity carries a status field ('draft' | 'revision' | 'final') that is wired through the backend (Prisma column, Zod enum validation on POST/PATCH, default 'draft') but has no UI consumer. Paper.tsx:88 destructures a status prop in SubRowProps and a comment promises 'The status chip is rendered separately because it sits on the right of the row' — but the function body never renders it. title: Chat/scene session picker: rename/delete icons hidden on touch
description: The session picker in the chat and scene tabs reveals rename/delete affordances on hover (desktop pattern), so on touch devices the icons are effectively invisible — users have no way to rename or delete a session from a phone/tablet. Investigate the picker UX and provide a touch-accessible affordance (always-visible icons on coarse pointers, long-press menu, or trailing kebab/overflow menu). Scope: SceneTab + ChatPanel session list rows.

title: [Outline] Wire onAddItem/onEditItem to a create/edit modal in EditorPage
description:
OutlineTab is rendered in the sidebar and drag-reorder works, but the create/edit affordances are stubs. EditorPage passes onAddItem={() => undefined} and onEditItem={() => undefined} to OutlineTab — no modal exists for creating a new outline item or editing an existing item's title/sub/status. Backend CRUD, encryption, ownership, and the frontend hook (useCreateOutlineMutation / useUpdateOutlineMutation) are all in place; only the modal + handlers need to be built. Likely mirrors the StoryModal or CharacterSheet edit pattern.

See: frontend/src/pages/EditorPage.tsx (onAddItem/onEditItem stubs), frontend/src/components/OutlineTab.tsx (consumes the callbacks), frontend/src/hooks/useOutline.ts (mutations already wired).

title: Decide whether e2e.yml should be PR-blocking now that T8 has shipped
description:
## Why

docs/done/done-T.md and playwright.config.ts both describe [T8] as 'tier-2 PR-blocking' E2E — but .github/workflows/e2e.yml still triggers on workflow_dispatch only. Its header comment and 'on:' block comment both still say 'until T8 ships real tests' / 'Once [T8] ships real E2E coverage, flip these on' — T8 (and T8.1) shipped real specs (tests/e2e/full-flow.spec.ts etc.). So either the triggers were never flipped (oversight) or manual-only is a deliberate later call (E2E runtime/flakiness/cost on every PR is a real tradeoff).

## What

Decide and act:
- (a) flip the push/pull_request triggers on so e2e.yml actually gates PRs as T8 intended, or
- (b) keep manual-only deliberately, and rewrite the comments to state *why* (not 'until T8 ships').

## Context

Surfaced during story-editor-at5's stale-comment audit of its touch-set. at5 fixes e2e.yml's comments to be *honest about the current state* (manual workflow_dispatch only) but deliberately does NOT make the trigger decision — that is CI policy, a different concern from 'eliminate the shared dev watcher'.
notes:
verify: design decision — set when this issue is planned (it is a CI-policy call, not a code change with an automated check)
spec-origin: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-eliminate-shared-dev-watcher-design.md (e2e.yml stale-comment audit finding)

title: Bundle story-editor-shared into the backend prod artifact (esbuild/tsup) — drop shared/dist + the exports source-condition
description:
## Why

After story-editor-at5, the backend dev server and both test suites resolve story-editor-shared from shared/src directly, but PROD still resolves shared/dist — the prod backend is a tsc-compiled CommonJS process and `import 'story-editor-shared'` must hit a resolvable package at runtime (tsc does not rewrite module specifiers). So shared/dist, the exports map, and the 'source' condition all still exist purely to serve the prod runtime.

The clean end state: bundle shared/src into the backend's prod artifact at build time (esbuild/tsup), so shared is inlined and there is NO story-editor-shared runtime specifier in prod at all — no shared/dist, no exports map, no source condition. shared is pure Zod/TS and bundles trivially; Prisma/argon2/pg/express etc. stay external.

## What

- Move the backend prod build from bare tsc to a bundler (esbuild or tsup) that inlines story-editor-shared while keeping native/heavy deps external.
- Drop shared/package.json's exports map and the 'source' condition added by at5; drop shared/dist from backend/Dockerfile's builder + runner stages.
- Keep dev/test on the plain resolve.alias (those survive at5 → D; only at5's exports-map work is superseded).
- Confirm: prod image builds, backend boots, Prisma codegen still resolves, source maps still emit, the dist/ layout the runner stage copies is still correct.

## Context

Surfaced while brainstorming story-editor-at5 — see docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-eliminate-shared-dev-watcher-design.md, section 'Forward pointer — Option D'. Deliberately scoped OUT of at5: at5 is 'restructure to kill the watcher'; this is a backend build-toolchain migration with its own review surface. Logically follows at5 (supersedes part of its work) — needs its own brainstorm + plan.

close_reason: Bundled story-editor-shared into the backend prod artifact via tsup: backend/dist/index.js is now a self-contained CJS bundle with no story-editor-shared runtime specifier. Dropped shared/dist, shared's build script, shared/tsconfig.build.json, and the exports map; shared/package.json main -> ./src/index.ts. Swapped the dev TS runner from ts-node-dev/ts-node to tsx (devDependency, never in prod). Slimmed backend/Dockerfile (no shared build step, no shared/dist COPY, no -w story-editor-shared install). Added CI bundle-inlining assertion + backend boot smoke. Verify ran end-to-end from clean: structural greps, 3 typechecks, tsup build + inline assertion, shared/backend/frontend test suites (77/927/972), tsx hot-reload, prod Docker image build+boot.

title: Bump postgres base image from 16 to 18 (current stable)
description:
docker-compose.yml pins postgres:16-alpine. PostgreSQL 18 is the current stable major (released Sept 2025); 17 is also available. PG 16 (Sept 2023) is two majors behind. It is NOT end-of-life — supported until Nov 2028 — so this is not urgent, but the repo otherwise tracks current stable versions (Node 24, etc.) and the DB image should not be the lone laggard.

Why this is its own issue and NOT folded into the dev-container Docker cleanup (story-editor-lki): a Postgres major bump is a data-directory migration, not a tag swap. PG 18 refuses to start on a PG-16 'pgdata' volume — it needs pg_upgrade or a dump/restore cycle. Unlike Node/nginx (stateless), Postgres has on-disk state whose format is major-version-specific.

Scope of the work:
- Decide 17 vs 18 (brainstorm gate).
- Update docker-compose.yml image tag (postgres:16-alpine -> postgres:NN-alpine).
- Handle the pgdata data-directory migration. If the app is still pre-deployment (CLAUDE.md implies so: 'no users, no stored content'), there is no production data — 'make reset-db' rebuilds local dev data and is sufficient. If deployed against real data later, a pg_upgrade or dump/restore procedure is required.
- Check SELF_HOSTING.md for any PG version reference and update it.
- Check scripts/backup-db.sh and scripts/backup-restore-drill.sh — they invoke pg_dump inside the container; confirm no version assumptions break.

Needs its own plan (brainstorm gate per CLAUDE.md).

title: [Frontend] Sidebar progress footer duplicates backend /progress math — consolidate
description:
GET /api/stories/:id/progress exists and is tested (backend/tests/routes/story-progress.test.ts) but has NO frontend consumer. Sidebar.tsx:110 computes the progress percentage client-side — Math.min(100, Math.max(0, Math.round((words/goal)*100))), fed by a client-side chapter word-count sum in EditorPage.tsx — while the backend endpoint does Math.floor((wordCount/targetWords)*100) with no clamp. Same concept, two implementations, different rounding and clamping.

Surfaced during the Story entity consolidation (story-editor-d7e), which adds storyProgressResponseSchema to story-editor-shared and wires respond() onto the endpoint as a backend egress guard — but leaves it consumerless.

Follow-up: add a useStoryProgress hook that fetches the endpoint and parses storyProgressResponseSchema, have Sidebar consume it and drop its own math. This is a behaviour change (rounding/clamping differ), so it needs its own verify line + tests. Alternative if the endpoint is judged genuinely unwanted: delete it and its tests instead.
close_reason: Mis-framed on filing. Investigation for story-editor-d7e found GET /api/stories/:id/progress has zero consumers — the frontend Sidebar footer derives wordCount/percent client-side from already-cached chapter data, live and better than the snapshot endpoint. 'Wiring the frontend onto it' would be a regression. Resolution: the dead endpoint + findTargetWords + listWordCountsForStory + story-progress.test.ts are deleted as part of story-editor-d7e (divergence #4 in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-story-entity-consolidation-design.md). No separate work remains.

title: Unify serialize* handler-boundary helpers into serializeNarrative(row, wireKeys)
description:
backend/src/lib/serialize.ts has three near-identical helpers converting repo-shape rows (Date timestamps, sometimes extra runtime columns) to wire shape (ISO strings, strict schema). serializeCharacter spreads ...row (safe — RepoCharacter carries no extra runtime keys); serializeMessage and serializeStory must explicitly pick because their runtime rows carry extra columns (chatId / userId resp.). That spread-vs-pick split is itself a mini-drift.

A single serializeNarrative(row, wireKeys) that always picks the given keys and converts createdAt/updatedAt would unify all three. Surfaced during story-editor-d7e; out of scope there (touches Character + Message serialization and their tests). Low priority — cosmetic consistency, no behaviour change.
close_reason: Resolved during story-editor-d7e investigation: a generic serializeNarrative(row, schema) helper is not worth it — it would trade per-field compile-time type-checking for Zod-schema reflection + 'as T', to fix what is only cosmetic inconsistency (three tiny co-located tested boundary converters; the spread-vs-pick split is structurally justified — it reflects whether the repo row carries extra columns the wire schema doesn't). Instead, story-editor-d7e does the cheap hardening: serializeCharacter is rewritten from spread to explicit-pick so all three helpers use the one safe pattern, with a stray-key test locking it. No generic helper. See docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-story-entity-consolidation-design.md (backend/src/lib/serialize.ts section + Out of scope).
